text: The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the ligament of Treitz from the left side. Cotillier forceps are used in robotic arms one and two, while hook electrocautery is employed in robotic arm four to dissect the peritoneum overlying the proximal jejunum. The bowel is retracted inferiorly using atraumatic graspers to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon preserves the inferior mesenteric vein, retracting it laterally and cephalad to prevent injury during dissection. The aorta is visible to the left of the proximal jejunum. The dissection continues as the thin peritoneal attachments of the bowel are divided beneath the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).

The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to take down adhesions from a previous laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The lateral wall of the duodenum is identified, and a combination of electrocautery and blunt dissection is employed to separate the duodenum from the transverse colon mesentery.
The surgeon medializes the duodenum, revealing the inferior vena cava. The dissection plane during the COCR maneuver is carefully maintained along the duodenum to prevent injury.

The surgeon mobilizes the third portion of the duodenum, proceeding cephalad along the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava. The duodenum is retracted medially to facilitate the removal of retroperitoneal attachments at the ligament of trites. A window is created posterior to the duodenum, revealing the aorta visible from the right side of the ligament of trites.

The surgeon completes the takedown of the ligament of trites and delivers the proximal jejunum through the retroperitoneum using Caudillere forceps and an assistant grasper. Electrocautery is then used to enlarge the retroperitoneal window by dissecting back towards the patient's left.

The surgeon mobilizes a vascularized omental flap using a ligature to divide the omentum. The omentum is then secured at the apex of the acute angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and the aorta, preventing the bowel from slipping back.
